2 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Anthropic recently published one of the most fascinating AI interpretability papers yet, titled Verbalizable Representations Form a Global Workspace in Language Models. Instead of focusing on making Claude bigger or faster, the researchers explored what happens inside the model before it generates a response. Using a technique called the Jacobian Lens (J-Lens), they identified an internal reasoning state called J-space (or Chain Space), where Claude appears to temporarily hold information while solving problems.
One of the most interesting experiments involved asking Claude, “A spider has ___ legs.” Before answering “8,” the internal representation contained the concept “spider.” Researchers replaced that hidden representation with “ant,” and Claude answered “6” without changing the prompt or editing the output. They modified the model’s internal reasoning, and the final answer changed accordingly.
Another experiment showed that while reading Spanish text, Claude internally recognized it as Spanish. After researchers changed that hidden representation to “French,” Claude claimed the text was French but still processed the Spanish correctly. This suggests that some abilities rely on the Chain Space, while others operate independently.
The researchers also removed the Chain Space entirely. Claude continued producing fluent English but largely lost its reasoning ability, indicating that language fluency and reasoning are supported by different internal mechanisms. You can read a complete technical breakdown of how these mechanisms operate over at Data Science Dojo or explore VentureBeat’s in-depth analysis of the study.
These findings resemble Bernard Baars’s Global Workspace Theory, which proposes that conscious thought arises from a small shared workspace while many other brain processes run in the background. However, Anthropic is clear that this does not mean Claude is conscious. The research simply shows that a workspace-like reasoning mechanism can emerge naturally during training.
For me, the biggest takeaway isn’t that Claude is “thinking” like a human. It’s that we’re finally beginning to understand what happens inside these models instead of treating them as complete black boxes. As AI continues to evolve, interpretability may become just as important as building larger and more powerful models.
